title: NYT slams Microsoft for building copyright-infringing supercomputer for OpenAI
gist: The New York Times has moved to amend its copyright lawsuit against OpenAI and Microsoft, alleging Microsoft intentionally built a bespoke supercomputer specifically to help OpenAI train AI models on NYT's copyrighted works without permission. The amendment responds to a new Supreme Court standard for contributory infringement that now requires plaintiffs to show intentional inducement of illegal conduct. NYT claims Microsoft's deployment of NYT-trained LLMs boosted its market capitalization by a trillion dollars in the past year alone.

themes.theme: NYT Legal Strategy Pivot After Supreme Court Ruling

insights.claim: The Supreme Court sided with Cox Communications in a case where Sony tried to claim Cox contributed to music piracy, establishing a new standard for contributory infringement that now requires plaintiffs to prove intentional inducement of illegal conduct.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: This new legal standard raises the bar for all future contributory copyright infringement cases, directly affecting the viability of lawsuits against AI infrastructure providers and platform hosts.
insights.quote: Moving forward, plaintiffs will have to prove that parties intentionally acted to induce illegal conduct.

insights.claim: The New York Times amended its copyright complaint against OpenAI and Microsoft specifically to align its contributory infringement claim with a new Supreme Court standard requiring proof of intentional inducement, signaling the NYT believes it has sufficient evidence of intent.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: NYT's willingness to update its legal theory rather than abandon the case suggests it has discovered strong evidence of intent during discovery, increasing litigation risk for Microsoft and OpenAI.
insights.quote: Today, we asked the court for permission to file an amended complaint that further strengthens our case, clarifying our claim of contributory infringement against Microsoft based on new law and new evidence uncovered during discovery.

insights.claim: NYT voluntarily dismissed two claims — contributory copyright infringement and trademark dilution — against all defendants in exchange for strengthening its remaining claims, a strategic streamlining to focus on its most potent arguments.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Narrowing claims typically signals plaintiff confidence in core arguments and reduces trial complexity, potentially accelerating a resolution that could set precedent for AI copyright liability.

themes.theme: Microsoft's Bespoke AI Supercomputer as Copyright Infringement Vehicle

insights.claim: NYT alleges Microsoft built a bespoke supercomputer ranked among the most powerful in the world specifically to help OpenAI train AI models on copyrighted works without permission, not merely providing generic cloud computing services.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: If the court accepts this framing, Microsoft faces direct liability as an infrastructure provider — a precedent that would expose all hyperscalers building AI-specific infrastructure to copyright infringement claims.
insights.quote: Microsoft specifically designed it for the purpose of using essentially the whole Internet—curated to disproportionately feature Times Works—to train the most capable LLM in history.

insights.claim: NYT alleges its articles were more heavily weighted in Microsoft's AI training supercomputer because both firms sought the highest-quality journalism to confidently mimic in model outputs, making NYT content a deliberate and disproportionate target.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: The 'deliberate weighting' allegation is key to meeting the new intentional inducement standard — if proven, it demonstrates active selection rather than passive hosting of infringing content.

insights.claim: NYT alleges that Microsoft's deployment of NYT-trained LLMs throughout its product line boosted Microsoft's market capitalization by a trillion dollars in the past year alone, framing copyright infringement as a direct driver of massive market value creation.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: The trillion-dollar market cap claim establishes the scale of alleged unjust enrichment, which could influence both damage calculations and settlement dynamics significantly in Microsoft's favor to resolve.
insights.quote: Microsoft's deployment of Times-trained LLMs throughout its product line helped boost its market capitalization by a trillion dollars in the past year alone.

themes.theme: ChatGPT as Paywall Bypass and NYT Market Harm Evidence

insights.claim: Discovery revealed that ChatGPT users were able to retrieve near-verbatim excerpts of NYT articles by requesting 'the next paragraph,' effectively using ChatGPT as a paywall bypass tool, which NYT presents as direct evidence of market substitution.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Documented paywall circumvention by users demonstrates concrete market harm from AI tools to subscription-based media businesses, strengthening legal and regulatory arguments for AI content restrictions.
insights.quote: In some cases, users told ChatGPT they were trying to skirt paywalls and were able to see significant chunks of articles by requesting to see the 'next paragraph.'
insights.locator: Section: Model outputs show market harms, NYT alleged

insights.claim: One of the earliest court verdicts finding AI training to be fair use was explicitly granted because plaintiffs failed to prove market harms, establishing that evidence of substitution is the critical battleground in AI copyright cases.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: NYT's strong substitution evidence (side-by-side comparisons, paywall bypass screenshots) positions it to potentially overcome the fair use defense that has protected other AI developers from liability.
insights.quote: Notably, one of the earliest verdicts finding that AI training was fair use was explicitly granted due to the plaintiffs' failure to prove market harms.
insights.locator: Section: Model outputs show market harms, NYT alleged

insights.claim: ChatGPT outputs summarizing Wirecutter reviews redirected affiliate link clicks away from the NYT, directly costing Wirecutter writers commission income — a specific, quantifiable harm beyond general market substitution.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: The affiliate link harm illustrates how AI summarization creates specific financial losses for media properties, a model applicable to any publisher with commerce or affiliate revenue streams.

themes.theme: AI Hallucinations as Reputational Copyright Harm

insights.claim: Microsoft Bing Chat and ChatGPT hallucinated fake quotes attributed to real figures like Moira Forbes and fabricated entirely fictional NYT articles, which NYT claims creates reputational harm by associating the newspaper with content it never published.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: AI hallucinations that falsely cite reputable news outlets create brand and reputational risk for publishers that is distinct from substitution harm, opening a second legal front against AI companies.
insights.locator: Section: Model outputs show market harms, NYT alleged

insights.claim: NYT argues that users asking a search engine what the Times has written should be directed to the actual article rather than receiving either an unauthorized copy or an inaccurate forgery — framing AI responses as legally equivalent to counterfeiting.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: If courts accept the 'forgery' framing, it would dramatically expand copyright liability for AI companies beyond verbatim reproduction to encompass any misleading impersonation of original sources.
insights.quote: Users who ask a search engine what The Times has written on a subject should be provided with neither an unauthorized copy nor an inaccurate forgery of a Times article, but a link to the article itself.
insights.locator: Section: Model outputs show market harms, NYT alleged

themes.theme: OpenAI and Microsoft Fair Use Defense and Litigation Risk

insights.claim: OpenAI maintains that AI training on publicly available copyrighted works is fair use because models 'transformed the material for a different use,' but a federal judge last June suggested the fair use question in AI training remains far from settled.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: The unresolved fair use question represents a material legal overhang for all AI companies whose models were trained on internet data, with an adverse ruling potentially requiring model retraining at enormous cost.
insights.quote: Our models empower innovation, are trained on publicly available data, and are grounded in fair use.
insights.locator: Section: Model outputs show market harms, NYT alleged — Drew Pusateri quote

insights.claim: Microsoft characterizes NYT's amended complaint as a 'last-ditch effort to save its claim from unfavorable precedent set in other recent rulings,' suggesting Microsoft believes recent case law has weakened NYT's legal position.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Microsoft's aggressive framing signals the company plans to fight rather than settle, extending litigation timelines and legal uncertainty for the AI industry's copyright exposure.
insights.quote: a last-ditch effort by the plaintiff to save its claim from unfavorable precedent set in other recent rulings.

insights.claim: The most extreme outcome of the NYT lawsuit, if it wins on substitution grounds, could require OpenAI and Microsoft to completely wipe their AI models and retrain them from scratch without NYT content.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Even a small probability of model wipeout represents a tail risk of catastrophic operational disruption and competitive setback for OpenAI and Microsoft's AI product lines worth monitoring for investors.
insights.quote: the most extreme outcome could require OpenAI and Microsoft to wipe models and start over.
insights.locator: Section: Model outputs show market harms, NYT alleged

insights.claim: NYT has requested permanent injunctive relief to prevent future infringement in addition to extensive damages, meaning even a partial legal victory could constrain how OpenAI and Microsoft use news content in future AI products.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Permanent injunctions against future use of copyrighted news in AI training would structurally disadvantage AI companies that have relied on journalism as a high-quality training data source.

themes.theme: Broader Implications for AI Industry and Media Economics

insights.claim: The NYT was the first major publisher to sue OpenAI in 2023, establishing it as the legal vanguard for the publishing industry's battle against AI companies over training data rights.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: NYT's pioneering legal role means its case outcomes will set precedents that affect every publisher's ability to seek compensation from AI companies, making this lawsuit a sector-defining event.

insights.claim: ChatGPT positioned itself as a substitute for NYT subscriptions by providing similar informational content, which NYT alleges caused direct market harm to its subscription business model.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: The substitution claim applies broadly to any AI assistant that summarizes or reproduces paid content, signaling subscription-dependent media companies face structural revenue threats from AI chatbots.
